Jeg så meg rundt, vi satte oss i bilen og kjørte videre. Som ung gutt var jeg ganske sjenert. Mange av disse tingene vil jeg bare la være usagt. Jeg var ganske sjenert, og det kan virke rart, men jeg likte egentlig ikke damer. Faktisk likte jeg ikke jenter i det hele tatt.
Min far pleide å gå ned til elven hvor folk drakk, og jeg så gifte kvinner der uten sine ektemenn, oppføre seg uekte. Jeg sa til meg selv: "Hvis det er slik de er, vil jeg aldri ha noe med dem å gjøre." Jeg bestemte meg for at jeg aldri skulle gifte meg eller ha noe med kvinner å gjøre; jeg skulle bli fanger. Jeg elsket jakt og fangst.

text:
And I guess you wonder how I ever got married. But I remember the first date I ever had, after I got to be about seventeen, eighteen years old. As all boys you know, you meet that first sweetheart, you know, eyes like a dove, and teeth like pearls, neck like a swan, and you know how it is: prettiest thing you ever seen in your life. And I met one. So I thought she was so pretty, my. And so the boy that lived pretty close to me, he said, "Tell you what…" He had a girlfriend too, and he said, "We'll get them together." They was in school, you know. Said, "We'll get them together, and I'll get dad's old Ford." And we had to jack the back end of it up, and put a little gasoline and crank it, you remember how you use… So we got enough to buy two gallons of gas. And we could ride a long time on that. So we got our girlfriends and was going to take them a riding.
And I never will forget that night we went out. And I stopped at a little place to get some sandwiches and some Cokes. And so, I remember when I went in and got the sandwiches and we came out, and we drank the Coke and ate the sandwiches. I was taking the bottles back. And that was just when the girls first begin to be smart alecks, start smoking and things. When I come back out, to my surprise, my little queen was setting there smoking a cigarette.
text_no:
Jeg antar at du lurer på hvordan jeg noen gang kunne gifte meg. Jeg husker min første date da jeg var rundt sytten eller atten år gammel. Som alle gutter møter man sin første kjærlighet, med øyne som duer, tenner som perler, hals som en svane; det vakreste du noen gang har sett. Jeg møtte en slik jente, og syntes hun var utrolig vakker.
En venn av meg som bodde i nærheten, hadde også en kjæreste. Han sa: "La oss samle dem. Jeg kan låne pappas gamle Ford." Vi måtte jekke opp bakenden og fylle litt bensin før vi kunne få den i gang, husker du hvordan man gjorde det? Vi sparte opp nok penger til to liter bensin, noe som rakk til en lang kjøretur. Så vi hentet kjærestene våre og dro ut på en biltur.
Jeg glemmer aldri den kvelden. Vi stoppet ved et lite sted for å kjøpe smørbrød og Coca-Cola. Jeg gikk inn for å hente dem, og vi satt ute og spiste og drakk. Da jeg tok med flaskene tilbake, la jeg merke til noe. Dette var på den tiden da jenter begynte å bli frekke og begynte å røyke. Til min store overraskelse satt min lille dronning der og røykte en sigarett.

text:
Well, I've always had my opinion of a cigarette smoking woman, and I never have changed it. It's the lowest thing she can do. That's right. Now, I'm not here to preach the Gospel to you; these men do that. But let me tell you something, sister: That's the worse fifth columnist that America's got. Doctors' statistics prove that eighty percent of the children if they're breast raised children, die when they're eighteen months age with nicotine poison. That's statistics, I get that from the government. And it's the nicotine [unclear words].
And here not long ago, few hours ago when I was eating my breakfast, I set in one of the little luncheon places here, and there set a lady there, fifty years old, with enough manicure on her face to … or ever what you call that stuff that, and all over her face, it would've painted a barn. And she was setting in there, and she had her cigarette in her hand going [Brother Branham illustrates.] I felt sorry for the woman, so degraded and polluted.
text_no:
Jeg har alltid hatt min mening om kvinner som røyker sigaretter, og den har ikke forandret seg. Det er det laveste hun kan gjøre. Det er rett. Nå er jeg ikke her for å forkynne Evangeliet for dere; disse mennene gjør det. Men la meg fortelle deg noe, søster: Det er den verste femtekolonisten Amerika har. Legevitenskapens statistikker viser at åtti prosent av barn som får morsmelk, dør når de er atten måneder gamle av nikotinforgiftning. Det er statistikk fra regjeringen. Og det er nikotinen.
Her for ikke lenge siden, bare få timer siden da jeg spiste frokost, satt jeg på et lite lunsjsted, og der satt en dame på femti år med nok sminke i ansiktet til å male en låve. Hun satt der med en sigarett i hånden og røykte [Bror illustrerer]. Jeg følte meg trist for kvinnen, så nedverdiget og forurenset.

text: Well, listen brother, don't you fear about Russia coming over here and whipping us. We're whipping ourselves. That's right. Our own morals are degrading us. It isn't the robin that pecks on the apple that hurts the apple; it's the worm at the core that kills the apple. That's where it's at now. Termites, they're eating our nation's foundation out. Now, don't get up and go out. Listen, I tell you. If you do, women, if you do use those things, for Jesus' sake, don't no more. Let this be the day and cease from right now on. For if the Angel of God, whose servant I am, if you're not thought any more by doing it, when you get to the gates of heaven than what it's revealed to me by Him, you'll certainly stand on the outside. Now, remember that. You accept me as God's prophet, you keep away from such things as that, if you expect to go into the gates of glory. That's right.
text_no: Bror, vær ikke redd for at Russland skal komme hit og overvinne oss. Vi ødelegger oss selv. Våre egne moralverdier forfaller. Det er ikke trosten som hakker på eplet som skader det; det er marken i kjernen som dreper eplet. Det er der vi er nå. Termitter ødelegger vårt nasjonale fundament. Nå, ikke gå ut. Hør på meg. Kvinner, hvis dere bruker slike ting, for Jesu skyld, slutt med det nå. La denne dagen være dagen dere slutter, fra og med nå. For hvis Guds Engel, som jeg er Hans tjener, har åpenbart dette for meg, vil dere stå utenfor himmelens porter hvis dere fortsetter. Husk det. Hvis dere aksepterer meg som Guds profet, hold dere unna slike ting hvis dere ønsker å komme inn gjennom herlighetens porter. Det er sannheten.

text:
I know when He met me when I was a little boy, the first thing He said to me, "Don't you never drink, smoke, or defile your body in any way; there'll be a work for you to do when you get older." Now, when the Angel met me in the bush down there, as you know of…
There that little thing was setting there, pretty little lady, smoking that cigarette. And I… She said, "Have a cigarette, Billy."
I said, "No, ma'am, I don't smoke."
And she said, "Now, you said you didn't dance." They want to go to a place to dance.
I said, "No."
Said, "You don't dance; you don't drink; you don't smoke," said, "what do you like to do?"
I said, "I like to hunt and fish…" [Blank spot on tape.]
text_no:
Jeg vet at da Han møtte meg som liten gutt, sa Han først til meg: "Ikke drikk, røyk eller krenk kroppen din på noen måte; det er et arbeid for deg å gjøre når du blir eldre." Så, da Engelen møtte meg i buskene der nede, som dere vet…
Der satt hun, en pen liten dame, og røykte en sigarett. Hun sa til meg: "Vil du ha en sigarett, Billy?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, frue, jeg røyker ikke."
Hun fortsatte: "Du sa at du ikke danser." De ville gå et sted for å danse.
Jeg svarte: "Nei."
Hun sa: "Du danser ikke; du drikker ikke; du røyker ikke; hva liker du å gjøre da?"
Jeg sa: "Jeg liker å jakte og fiske..." [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd.]

text:
I just got up, and went up over the hill, and set down up there in a field that night, the moon shining down; I said, "I can't have friends; I'm a black sheep among the boys. And Lord, somebody, let me die. I don't want to live like this. I'm a prisoner and I don't know what to do."
But what I see now, is all in God's great program. I might've lost a lot of friends then, but He's giving it back ten thousand times more today, by doing that what was right, to holding on to Him. And I said, "Oh, I don't know. I just might as well try to end life."
And a little later on, I remember then, I went on, thought, "Well, I'll stick it out." Time passed; many things happened.
text_no:
Jeg sto opp og gikk over åsen, satte meg ned på et jorde den natten mens månen skinte. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg har ingen venner; jeg er et svart får blant guttene. Herre, la meg dø. Jeg vil ikke leve slik. Jeg er en fange og vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre." 
Men nå ser jeg at det var en del av Guds store plan. Jeg mistet kanskje mange venner da, men ved å holde fast på Ham, har Han gitt meg tusen ganger mer tilbake i dag. Jeg sa: "Å, jeg vet ikke. Jeg kan like gjerne ende livet."
Senere husker jeg at jeg tenkte: "Vel, jeg får bare holde ut." Tiden gikk, og mange ting skjedde.

text:
When I got married… I met this girl. I'm so happy, my boy setting present this afternoon to know this about his mother. She was a lady, every ounce of it. She was a Christian girl. And I met her, and she was very nice. And I started going with her. And she neither smoked, drank, danced, or went to places like that. So, only thing we would do, we would go riding at night, and we had to be in at a certain time, nine o'clock. And I'd come back; she's very lady, nice parents.
So I thought how nice she was, but her father was a organizer on the Pennsylvania Railroad, making about five hundred and something dollars a month, during the time of the depression. I made twenty cents an hour digging ditches. What's something to take a girl like that? But she was very lovely. Her grave today has some flowers that I just planted on it. She lies beneath there, her body. Her soul's in glory with my baby. I love her yet today with all my heart. And she's… What a lovely person.
text_no:
Da jeg giftet meg, møtte jeg denne jenta. Jeg er så glad, spesielt når jeg tenker på hvordan min sønn ser opp til sin mor. Hun var virkelig en dame med alt det innebærer. Hun var en kristen jente. Da vi møttes, var hun veldig hyggelig, og vi begynte å gå ut sammen. Hun røykte ikke, drakk ikke, danset ikke eller gikk på slike steder. Det eneste vi gjorde, var å kjøre rundt på kveldene, og vi måtte være hjemme til en bestemt tid, klokken ni. Jeg kom tilbake, og hun var alltid en dame med hyggelige foreldre.
Jeg tenkte på hvor fin hun var, men hennes far var organisasjonsarbeider på Pennsylvania Railroad og tjente omtrent fem hundre dollar i måneden under depresjonen. Jeg tjente tjue cent i timen med å grave grøfter. Hva kunne jeg tilby en jente som henne? Men hun var utrolig kjærlig. I dag ligger det blomster på hennes grav som jeg nylig plantet. Hun hviler der under jorden, mens hennes sjel er i herlighet sammen med vår baby. Jeg elsker henne fortsatt av hele mitt hjerte. For en vidunderlig person hun var.

text:
And she came into my life. And I knew it got to a place where I had to either say, "Now, you must…" I must marry her, or I must let her alone, let her somebody. A girl like that, a lady will certainly find somebody that'll be good to her, and I didn't make enough money to take care of her." So I said, "Well, the only thing I got to do: I've got to tell her good-bye and let some other boy that can take care of her…" I loved her enough, even though I had to sacrifice being with her, let somebody take a hold, take her that would take care of her and make her a good living.
So I tried to make up my mind, and I said, "Well, I… Maybe I can. Maybe I could make a living for her." I said, "It's so hard to do, to ask her that." So finally I thought, "How will I ask her?" And I guess you wonder how I did. I… For about a month I tried to get up enough nerve to do it. I don't know whether you brethren have that much trouble or not, but I had an awful time. And I'd look at her, and I thought she was pretty, and she was a good girl. And why do… I thought, "Oh couldn't we be happy together. We might not have very much, but we could be happy." And so I thought, "How will I do it?"
text_no:
Hun kom inn i livet mitt, og jeg visste at jeg var nødt til å ta et valg: Enten måtte jeg gifte meg med henne, eller så måtte jeg la henne gå, slik at hun kunne finne noen andre. En slik jente, en så flott kvinne, ville helt sikkert finne noen som kunne være god mot henne, og jeg tjente ikke nok penger til å ta vare på henne. Så jeg sa til meg selv: "Vel, det eneste jeg kan gjøre, er å si farvel og la en annen gutt, som kan forsørge henne, ta henne." Jeg elsket henne nok til å gi avkall på å være sammen med henne, slik at noen andre kunne gi henne et godt liv.
Jeg prøvde å bestemme meg og tenkte: "Kanskje jeg kan. Kanskje jeg kunne klare å forsørge henne." Det var så vanskelig å spørre henne om det. Til slutt tenkte jeg: "Hvordan skal jeg spørre henne?" Jeg antar du lurer på hvordan jeg gjorde det. I omtrent en måned prøvde jeg å skaffe meg nok mot til å gjøre det. Jeg vet ikke om dere brødre har hatt like mye problemer med dette, men jeg hadde det veldig vanskelig. Jeg så på henne, og jeg syntes hun var vakker og en god jente. Jeg tenkte: "Kunne vi ikke vært lykkelige sammen? Vi ville kanskje ikke hatt mye, men vi kunne vært lykkelige." Så jeg tenkte: "Hvordan skal jeg gjøre det?"

text:
So I'd try to ask her, you know, and I… You know how you feel, that real funny feeling, you know, and I'd just… It'd choke me up, and I couldn't do it. So you know how I asked her to marry me? I wrote her a letter and asked if she'd marry me. And so I…
Now, it wasn't Dear Miss, it was just a little more, what do you call that, mushy, than that, you know? And I asked her if she'd marry me. And I wrote it all out one night, you know, and I put it in a letter; and the next day I was going to work, so I dropped it in the box. And I know I was going to take her to church Sunday night, or Wednesday night.
text_no:
Jeg forsøkte å spørre henne, men du vet hvordan man får den merkelige følelsen, og jeg… Det knep seg sammen i halsen, og jeg klarte det ikke. Vet du hvordan jeg spurte henne om å gifte seg med meg? Jeg skrev et brev og spurte om hun ville gifte seg med meg.
Nå, det begynte ikke med «Kjære Frøken», det var litt mer, hva skal jeg si, romantisk enn det. Jeg spurte om hun ville gifte seg med meg og skrev det hele ned en kveld. Jeg la brevet i en konvolutt og tok det med meg neste dag på vei til jobb, og jeg slapp det i postkassen. Jeg visste at jeg skulle ta henne med til menigheten søndag kveld eller onsdag kveld.

text:
And so, when Wednesday night begin to come I begin to get nervous, because I forgot and put it in the box and thought, "What if her mother got a hold of it?" Now, her mother's a fine woman, and she may be here this afternoon. And I'm not saying this for any harm, but her dad was just a real good; he's a real German, Brumbach. And her mother was Scot, and so I could get along with Charlie all right, her dad. But her mother and I just--we didn't see things the way we ought to. She was kindly, you know, kind of little classical, and I was just one of these here plow boys. So I thought, "Well now, maybe what if her mother got a hold of that, oh, my. I'll get something when I go up there."
So come Wednesday I was so nervous I couldn't hardly go up. I had an old backslidden Ford, and it really was backslidden, that one was. And I could almost make forty miles an hour in it. That was twenty miles this way and twenty miles up-and-down this way, you know.
text_no:
Da onsdag kveld nærmet seg, begynte jeg å bli nervøs fordi jeg hadde glemt og lagt esken tilbake og tenkte: «Hva om moren hennes fikk tak i den?» Nå er moren hennes en flott kvinne, og hun kan være her i ettermiddag. Jeg sier ikke dette for å skade noen, men faren hennes var en god mann, en ekte tysker, Brumbach. Moren hennes var skotsk, så jeg kom godt overens med Charlie, faren hennes. Men moren og jeg så ikke alltid ting på samme måte. Hun var litt mer klassisk, mens jeg var en enkel gårdsgutt. Så jeg tenkte: «Hva om moren hennes fikk tak i det, å nei. Jeg vil få høre det når jeg drar opp dit.»
Da onsdagen kom, var jeg så nervøs at jeg nesten ikke klarte å dra. Jeg hadde en gammel nedslitt Ford, og den var virkelig nedslitt. Jeg klarte så vidt førti mil i timen i den. Det var tjue mil denne veien og tjue mil opp og ned på veien, vet du.

text:
So I remember, it was that Ford that I visit Brother John Ryan for the first time in Dowagiac, Michigan; you remember this story, Brother Ryan. So I remember, I thought, "Oh, my." I believe this is the first time Brother Ryan ever set present when I was telling my life story, far as I know of. This is going to include him in a few moments.
And then I remember at the… I thought, "Well now, what will I do if her mother got a hold of that; there's going to be some trouble."
So when it come Wednesday night I drove up out front; I knowed better than to blow the horn, for I think this boys, if the girl is worth going with, is worth going in and get her (that's right.), not set out front and blow the horn.
So I went up to the door and I knocked on the door, and she come to the door, and she opened the door; she said, "Why, good evening, Billy," she said, "come in."
I thought, "Oh, oh, going to get me in there now, and shut the door, and then I would be in an awful fix, getting me in the house." I thought… I said, "Thank you, Hope," I said, "may I set out here on the porch?"
Said, "Oh, no, come on in."
I thought, "Oh, me." So I walked in, and held my hat in my hand, I said, "You about ready to go to church?"
She said, "Just in a few moments." She said, "Mother, would you talk," oh, "to Bill while I finish in there?"
Oh, my, she come, Mrs. Brumbach, come in and set down. And oh, talk about perspiring. I said, "Sure fine weather."
"Yes, it is, Billy."
Set there a little bit; I thought that girl never would get ready. And so, after while she come out; she said, "It's such a lovely evening, let's walk down to the church."
I thought, "Oh, oh. There's goes the time." I thought, "I'd better look real good, because this is the last time I get to be with you. So, I know that. I done had it… You know how Satan will lie to you. He will have you believing anything." I thought "This is all; she's going to tell me the finish."
So I went down and I never did hear what that preacher said that night. I was setting looking at her; I just thinking about how pretty she was, and how nice she was, and how I hoped she would get somebody would be good to her. And I looked at her, and I thought, "My." And Brother Davis is up preaching, you know, and I never heard what he said. He dismissed, and went outside; I thought, "Now's when I get it."
text_no:
Jeg husker godt den gangen jeg besøkte Bror John Ryan for første gang i Dowagiac, Michigan; du husker sikkert denne historien, Bror Ryan. Jeg husker at jeg tenkte: "Åh, det var sikkert første gang Bror Ryan noen gang satte seg ned for å høre meg fortelle livshistorien min, så vidt jeg vet." Dette kommer til å inkludere ham om noen øyeblikk.
Jeg minnes også at jeg tenkte: "Nå, hva vil jeg gjøre hvis moren hennes finner ut av dette; det kommer til å bli trøbbel."
Da onsdag kveld kom, kjørte jeg opp foran huset hennes. Jeg visste bedre enn å tute, for jeg mener at om en jente er verdt å gå ut med, er hun verdt å gå inn og hente (det er riktig)—ikke bare sitte utenfor og tute. Så jeg gikk opp til døren og banket på. Hun kom for å åpne og sa: "God kveld, Billy, kom inn."
Jeg tenkte: "Å nei, nå skal de få meg inn i huset." Jeg sa: "Takk, Hope, kan jeg vente her på verandaen?"
"Nei, kom inn," svarte hun.
Jeg tenkte: "Å, hjelpes meg." Så jeg gikk inn, holdt hatten i hånden og spurte: "Er du klar til å dra til menigheten?"
"Helt om litt," svarte hun. "Mor, kan du snakke med Bill mens jeg gjør meg ferdig?"
Å, du milde. Mrs. Brumbach kom inn og satte seg. Jeg svettet som aldri før. Jeg sa: "Det er fint vær."
"Ja, det er det, Billy."
Vi satt der en stund; jeg trodde aldri den jenta skulle bli ferdig. Til slutt kom hun ut og sa: "Det er en så nydelig kveld, la oss gå til menigheten."
Jeg tenkte: "Å, der går tiden." Jeg tenkte at jeg skulle se nøye på henne, for jeg visste at dette var siste gang jeg fikk være sammen med henne. Du vet hvordan Satan kan lyve og få deg til å tro hva som helst. Jeg tenkte: "Dette er det hele; hun skal avslutte det med meg."
Så gikk vi til menigheten, og jeg hørte aldri hva forkynneren sa den kvelden. Jeg satt bare og så på henne, tenkte på hvor vakker og snill hun var, og håpet at hun ville finne noen som var god mot henne. Jeg så på henne og tenkte: "Åh, min." Og Bror Davis sto der og forkynte, men jeg fikk ikke med meg hva han sa. Da møtet ble avsluttet og vi gikk ut, tenkte jeg: "Nå får jeg høre det."

text:
We started walking home, moonlight night, you know, we'd walk under those shades of the trees, you know, when you come out she come out she had real dark brown eyes, when she'd look around, I could just… You know how you feel, that funny feeling. Now, you all did the same thing [unclear words] that's right. I just admit mine. Ha-ha-ha. Now, isn't that right? Sure, raise up your hand. Now, that's better. Yes, sir.
That feeling you know, and I thought, "Oh, my," why, I thought after we got close to home, I thought maybe she forgot it, never got the letter, you know. And I thought it got stuck in the mailbox. I got pretty game then; I was talking pretty lively going down the street. And I just talk about next Sunday night again, you know, come walking down the street. Why, I was feeling fine. And just about the time we got about a city block from the home, she said, "Billy."
And I said, "Yes?"
She said, "I got your letter."
Oh, my, There it went again. I said, "Uh, uh, You did?"
She said, "Uh-huh."
text_no:
Vi begynte å gå hjem i måneskinnet. Vi gikk under trærnes skygger. Hun hadde mørkebrune øyne, og da hun så seg rundt, fikk jeg den merkelige følelsen. Dere kjenner den følelsen, ikke sant? Alle har opplevd det samme. Jeg innrømmer min. Ha-ha. Er det ikke sant? Så klart, løft hånden. Det er bedre. Ja, sir.
Den følelsen, og jeg tenkte: "Åh, min," kanskje hun hadde glemt det, aldri fikk brevet. Jeg trodde det satt fast i postkassen. Jeg ble modig og pratet i vei mens vi gikk nedover gaten. Jeg snakket om neste søndagskveld igjen, og følte meg fin. Da vi var omtrent en kvartal fra huset, sa hun plutselig: "Billy."
Jeg svarte: "Ja?"
Hun sa: "Jeg fikk brevet ditt."
Åh, der gikk det igjen. Jeg sa: "Eh, eh, gjorde du?"
Hun sa: "Ja."

text:
Well, I kept on walking; nobody said nothing. And I thought, "Woman, say something." You know how a woman can keep you in suspense like that, you know. Well, I thought, "Surely, a man ought to say amen then." Be a brave man. Ha-ha-ha. So then she said… just… I thought, "What will I do?" And she wouldn't say a word. And I thought, "Well, I've got to say something, because we're just a few doors from her house." And I said, "Did you read it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." That's all she said.
I thought, "Oh, say something, and tell me; I--I can't come back or run me away, or do something, because I'm--I'm under a big strain here." I said, "Did you read all of it?"
She said, "Uh-huh."
I said, "What'd you think of it?"
She said, "It was all right," and we got married." Ha-ha-ha. That's the way it happened. Married over here at Fort Wayne, Indiana.
text_no:
Jeg fortsatte å gå, men ingen sa noe. Jeg tenkte: "Kvinne, si noe." Du vet hvordan en kvinne kan holde deg i spenning, ikke sant? Så tenkte jeg: "Sikkert, en mann burde si amen da." Vær en modig mann. Ha-ha-ha. Så sa hun bare... Jeg tenkte: "Hva skal jeg gjøre?" Men hun sa ikke et ord. Da tenkte jeg: "Vel, jeg må si noe, for vi er bare noen få dører unna huset hennes." Så jeg sa: "Leste du det?"
Hun svarte bare: "Uh-huh." Det var alt hun sa.
Jeg tenkte: "Åh, si noe, og fortell meg; jeg--jeg kan ikke komme tilbake, eller jag meg bort, eller gjør noe, fordi jeg--jeg er under et stort press her." Så jeg spurte: "Leste du hele greia?"
Hun sa: "Uh-huh."
Jeg spurte: "Hva syntes du om det?"
Hun sa: "Det var greit," og vi giftet oss. Ha-ha-ha. Sånn skjedde det. Vi giftet oss her i Fort Wayne, Indiana.

text:
And so we got married. And I never will forget when she told me then I had to ask her parents for her, said that was just… Oh, my, here it all… I thought I got by good there, but here that was before me. And I said, "Look, Hope," I said, "You know I believe we're supposed to be fifty-fifty." See? I said, "I'm supposed to be… We're going fifty-fifty on these things," I said, "let's just start it right now, what do you say?"
She said, "What do you mean?"
I said, "You being the girl, I believe it'd be best for you to ask your mother, and me being the boy, I'd ask your dad."
She said, "Very well."
I said, "Yeah. Well, you let me ask your dad first, will you?" If I get his promise, you know, first…
She said, "Well, you ask him tonight."
text_no:
Så vi giftet oss. Jeg vil aldri glemme øyeblikket da hun sa at jeg måtte spørre foreldrene hennes om hennes hånd. Det var da det virkelig slo meg. Jeg trodde jeg hadde kommet unna med det lett, men nå stod utfordringen rett foran meg. Jeg sa, "Hør her, Hope," sa jeg, "Jeg tror vi skal dele alt likt, femti-femti." Ser du? Jeg sa, "Vi skal gjøre det slik, la oss begynne med det nå, hva sier du?"
Hun spurte: "Hva mener du?"
Jeg svarte, "Du er jenta, så jeg tror det er best at du spør din mor, og siden jeg er gutten, spør jeg din far."
Hun sa, "Greit."
Jeg sa, "Ja. La meg spørre din far først, kan jeg?" Dersom jeg får hans samtykke først, vet du…
Hun svarte, "Du kan spørre ham i kveld."

text:
Well, I just couldn't do it that night after going through all that. So then I waited, next time I went up we were, went back. I set back there on the porch with her awhile, and we come in, her daddy was setting typing. And we walked in the house, and she said, "You'd better ask dad tonight because we got to make ready."
And I said, "Yes, that's--that's right."
So, I went in, and he was setting there typing, and I talked to her mother a little bit, you know, and looked around. I started out, and she looked at me; and I said… I motioned to her; I hadn't forgot it. So I said, "Mr. Brumbach?"
Said, "Yes, Bill."
I said, "Could--could I talk to you a little bit?"
He said, "Yes, go ahead." And he turned around.
I said, "I--I mean out on the porch. I seen him look around to Mrs. Brumbach. I thought, "Oh, oh, here it is." So I walked out to the porch, and he walked out there. I said… I just couldn't say it; I just couldn't get them wor--I'd just get real weak every time I go try to say something, you know. I said, "Sure is a pretty night, isn't it Charlie?"
He said, "Yes, it is, Bill." Set there a little while.
I said, "Been awful warm."
He said, "Yes," said, "you can have her, Bill." Ha-ha-ha. Why, I love him today.
I said, "You mean it?"
He said, "Yeah, I do."
Oh, my, I wanted to hug him right then. Said, "You can have her."
text_no:
Jeg klarte bare ikke å gjøre det den kvelden etter alt som hadde skjedd. Så jeg ventet til neste gang, da vi dro tilbake. Jeg satt med henne på verandaen en stund, og vi gikk inn. Faren hennes satt og skrev på skrivemaskinen sin. Da vi kom inn i huset, sa hun: "Du burde spørre pappa i kveld, for vi må forberede oss."
Jeg svarte: "Ja, det er rett."
Så jeg gikk bort til ham, som fortsatt satt og skrev. Jeg snakket litt med moren hennes og kikket rundt. På vei ut, så hun på meg, og jeg ga henne et tegn som antydet at jeg ikke hadde glemt det. Så sa jeg: "Mr. Brumbach?"
Han svarte: "Ja, Bill."
Jeg spurte: "Kan jeg prate litt med deg?"
Han sa: "Ja, vær så god," og snudde seg rundt.
Jeg sa: "Jeg mener på verandaen." Jeg så ham se bort på Mrs. Brumbach og tenkte: "Å nei, nå kommer det." Vi gikk ut på verandaen. Jeg prøvde å finne ordene, men ble bare svak hver gang jeg forsøkte å si noe. Jeg sa: "Det er en vakker kveld, ikke sant, Charlie?"
Han svarte: "Ja, det er det, Bill." Vi satt stille en stund.
Jeg sa: "Det har vært veldig varmt."
Han svarte: "Ja," og sa deretter: "Du kan få henne, Bill." Ha-ha-ha. Jeg elsker ham så mye i dag.
Jeg spurte: "Mener du det?"
Han svarte: "Ja, det gjør jeg."
Å, jeg ville klemme ham der og da. Han sa: "Du kan få henne."

text:
I said, "Look, Charlie," I said, "I know you give her a good home." I said, "You can get her anything she wants; I can't." I said, "I'm only making a small wage." But I said, "Charlie, she couldn't find anybody that thinks any more of her." And I said, "I'll work as long as there's breath in my body to work, and make her a living. And I'll do everything that I can to make her a living."
I never will forget; he's gone on too now. But he laid his hand over on my shoulder, and he said, "Bill, I'd rather you'd have her, and I know you love her, and I know she loves you. I rather you'd have her, then somebody maybe had plenty and wouldn't be good to her." Said, "After all, life doesn't consist of how much the world's goods you own, but how contented you are with the portion that's allotted to you." That's right, too [unclear words].
I said, "Thank you, Charlie. I'll do all that I can."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Se, Charlie, jeg vet at du gir henne et godt hjem. Du kan gi henne alt hun ønsker; det kan ikke jeg. Jeg tjener bare en liten lønn." Men jeg sa, "Charlie, hun kunne ikke ha funnet noen som bryr seg mer om henne enn meg." Og jeg sa, "Jeg vil arbeide så lenge det er pust i meg for å forsørge henne. Jeg vil gjøre alt jeg kan for å gi henne et liv."
Jeg vil aldri glemme det; han er også gått bort nå. Men han la hånden på skulderen min og sa, "Bill, jeg foretrekker at du har henne, for jeg vet at du elsker henne, og jeg vet at hun elsker deg. Jeg foretrekker at du har henne fremfor noen som kanskje har rikelig, men som ikke ville være god mot henne." Han sa, "Til syvende og sist består ikke livet av hvor mye materielle goder du eier, men hvor tilfreds du er med delen som er tildelt deg." Det er også riktig.
Jeg sa, "Takk, Charlie. Jeg skal gjøre alt jeg kan."

text:
Well, we got married; we moved into a little two room place that we rented. I never will forget what we went housekeeping on. Many of you remember the depression, don't you? Oh, my. [Blank spot on tape.] About two dollars, is what the stove cost me. I went down to Sears and Roebucks and got a breakfast set that hadn't been painted, and I painted it. And I put big shamrocks all over it. I was [unclear words]. She's German and me Irish, you know, so I said, "We'll make that, paint it red with big green shamrocks on it, just … we just… We were so happy. We didn't have much of the world's goods, but we were happy. It was home.
I remember the first time that I went then… We were real happy, she was working at a shirt factory, and we were trying to get enough money together to get us some furniture. And we'd been married, oh, several months. After about a year, little Billy Paul come on the scene. Oh, she almost died. And how I walked up-and-down the floors when the little fellow was being borned. And just as soon as he was born, I heard him cry, and I screamed; I said, "Thank You, Lord, it's a boy, and his name shall be called Billy Paul." [Blank spot on tape.]
text_no:
Vi giftet oss og flyttet inn i en liten to-roms leilighet som vi leide. Jeg vil aldri glemme hva vi startet husholdningen med. Mange av dere husker depresjonen, ikke sant? Å, min. [Tomt område på lydbånd.] Komfyren kostet meg omtrent to dollar. Jeg dro til Sears og Roebucks og kjøpte et ubehandlet frokostsett som jeg selv malte. Jeg pyntet det med store kløverblader. Jeg er irsk, og hun er tysk, så vi malte det rødt med store grønne kløverblader. Vi var så glade. Vi hadde ikke mye av verdens goder, men vi var lykkelige. Det var hjemmet vårt.
Jeg husker første gangen vi var der ... Vi var virkelig lykkelige. Hun jobbet på en skjortefabrikk, og vi prøvde å spare nok penger til å kjøpe møbler. Vi hadde vært gift i flere måneder, og etter omtrent ett år kom lille Billy Paul til verden. Hun var nær ved å dø. Jeg gikk frem og tilbake på gulvet mens den lille gutten ble født. Så snart jeg hørte ham gråte, ropte jeg: "Takk, Herre, det er en gutt, og hans navn skal være Billy Paul." [Tomt område på lydbånd.]

text:
Doctor came out in a few minutes, said, "Well, reverend, I'll charge you for this linoleum you wore out all up-and-down here," he said, "but you got a boy."
I said, "Yes, his name's Billy Paul."
And I become acquainted with Brother Ryan during that time; I met him at a service one day, and heard him testify in Louisville. So, he invited me, and that's when he come to my house, and he set there one day. Now, Pentecost was a strange thing to me, and how many times he tried to tell me about it. And he was setting there, and he just raised up; he threw up his hand and begin to speak with an unknown tongue. And he stopped, looked at me right straight, walked to me, laid his hand over on my shoulder, and said, "Brother Billy, you're just a lad now; there's a lot of youth to you yet. But someday that's going settle down, and Almighty God is going to use you to stir the nations." He walked out.
text_no:
Etter noen minutter kom legen ut og sa: "Vel, pastor, jeg må belaste deg for dette linoleumsgulvet du har slitt ned her," men så la han til, "du har fått en gutt."
Jeg svarte: "Ja, hans navn er Billy Paul."
I denne perioden ble jeg kjent med Bror Ryan; jeg møtte ham på et møte en dag, og hørte ham vitne i Louisville. Han inviterte meg, og det var da han besøkte meg hjemme. Nå, pinsebevegelsen var en fremmed ting for meg, og mange ganger prøvde han å forklare det for meg. Mens han satt der, reiste han seg plutselig, løftet hånden og begynte å tale i tunger. Han stoppet, så rett på meg, gikk bort, la hånden på skulderen min og sa: "Bror Billy, du er bare en gutt nå; det er fortsatt mye ungdom i deg. Men en dag vil dette forandre seg, og Allmektige Gud vil bruke deg til å vekke nasjonene." Så gikk han.

text:
There's the man setting there now that did it. I love the old fellow. And he went away, went to his home, and I'd visit his home. I remember we saved our money; I never will forget how much money I had; it's six or seven dollars to make the trip, that we saved. I was tired; I'd been, as a minister and was preaching, had the little Tabernacle there. I went on a vacation; I went to see Brother Ryan, went to Dowagiac. And he, we went fishing out on the lake. On the road back I was coming home and I come down through Mishawaka. And that was my first time to ever get acquainted with the denominational Pentecostal people.
And I passed by, at I believe Mishawaka, I guess, and there was a big tabernacle there, and the people was all crowded out on the streets and everywhere, they was… I thought, "What is this?" And I seen they had "Jesus Saves" and everything on the back of their cars. So I pulled my old Ford up and stopped, and thought, "What is this?"
text_no:
Det sitter en mann der nå som gjorde det. Jeg elsker den gamle mannen. Han dro hjem, og jeg besøkte ham hjemme. Jeg husker vi sparte penger; jeg vil aldri glemme hvor mye penger jeg hadde; det var seks eller syv dollar for å gjøre turen. Jeg var sliten, jeg hadde vært forkynner og holdt møter i den lille tabernaklet der. Jeg tok en ferie og dro for å besøke Bror Ryan i Dowagiac. Vi dro ut og fisket på innsjøen. På vei hjem igjen kom jeg gjennom Mishawaka. Det var første gang jeg ble kjent med de konfesjonelle pinsemenighetene.
Da jeg passerte Mishawaka, tror jeg, var det en stor tabernakel der, og folk var overalt på gatene. Jeg tenkte: "Hva er dette?" Jeg så at de hadde "Jesus frelser" og lignende slagord på bilene sine. Så jeg stoppet den gamle Forden min og tenkte: "Hva er dette?"

text:
And I went in and seen what it was, and it was a religious service. But oh, did I ever see people that didn't have manners. They were screaming, and shouting, and jumping, and that was awful for a Baptist. So I seen them people how they act; I thought, "Isn't that awful? Well, they haven't got any church manners at all."
So, but there was something got a hold of me. And so I… That night I wanted to stay all night, and I didn't… I counted my money, and I had enough money to get enough gasoline to get home. And I went down and bought me some stale rolls. And I knowed I could stay a couple days on that. So I didn't have any room, no money for a room, so I went out in a cornfield to sleep that night.
But however, they asked all the preachers come to the platform, and they was having a conference. And then, that night he said, "All the preachers here, we haven't got time for you to preach, but we want you to just raise up and say your name and where you're from. When it come mine I said, "Billy Branham, evangelist, Jeffersonville, Indiana," Sat down.
text_no:
Jeg gikk inn og så at det var en religiøs sammenkomst. Men jeg hadde aldri opplevd så dårlige manerer før. Folk skrek, ropte og hoppet, noe som var uvanlig for en baptist. Jeg så hvordan de oppførte seg og tenkte: "Er ikke dette forferdelig? De har ingen kirkeetikette."
Likevel var det noe som grep tak i meg. Den kvelden ønsket jeg å bli værende, men jeg telte pengene mine og hadde bare nok til bensin for å komme hjem. Jeg gikk ned og kjøpte noen gamle rundstykker, og visste at jeg kunne klare meg på dem et par dager. Jeg hadde ingen penger til et rom, så jeg sov i en maisåker den natten.
De ba alle predikantene komme opp på plattformen for en konferanse. Om kvelden sa de: "Alle predikanter her, vi har ikke tid til at dere kan preke, men vi vil at dere skal reise dere og si navnet deres og hvor dere kommer fra." Da det ble min tur, reiste jeg meg og sa: "Billy Branham, evangelist, Jeffersonville, Indiana," og satte meg ned.

text:
So then there been many of the young ministers preaching that day, but when they brought the minister out that night to preach, a colored brother, and he was real old, and he had just a little rim of white hair around the back of his head, and a great big old long preachers coat on, big velvet collar to it. Poor old fellow come out walking like this. And he got there, and first time I'd ever seen a microphone. And he was preaching, just started to speaking; he took his text over there: Job, I believe, 7 or 8, somewhere there, "Where was you when I laid the foundations of the world? Morning stars sing together, and sons of God shouted for joy…"
And the old fellow, I felt so sorry for him; I wanted to go up and hold his arms to keep him from falling while he was preaching, and he was so old. And I thought, "Why didn't they put some of them young fellows up here?" They'd been preaching all day, you know, about what Jesus done.
text_no:
Den dagen hadde mange unge predikanter talt, men om kvelden brakte de frem en eldre, farget bror til å forkynne. Han var virkelig gammel, hadde bare en liten krans av hvitt hår rundt bakhodet, og bar en stor, gammel predikantkåpe med en stor fløyelskrage. Den stakkars gamle mannen kom gående forsiktig fremover. Dette var første gangen jeg så en mikrofon. Han begynte å forkynne, og tok sin tekst fra Job, kapittel 7 eller 8: "Hvor var du da Jeg grunnla jorden? Morgenstjernene jublet sammen, og Guds sønner ropte av glede."
Jeg følte meg så lei meg for ham; jeg ønsket å gå opp og holde armene hans for å hindre ham fra å falle mens han forkynte, han var så gammel. Jeg tenkte: "Hvorfor satte de ikke noen av de unge karene her oppe?" De hadde jo prekt hele dagen om hva Jesus hadde gjort.

text:
But he went back yonder from about, and brought Him back across the skies like this, and down the horizontal rainbow in the second coming. And about the time he got down there, that old man hollered, "Whoopie," jumped up and clicked his heels together, went tipping off the platform, said, "You ain't got enough room up here for me to preach."
I looked at that, I thought, "Brother, if that Holy Ghost will make an old man act like that, what would it do for me? That's what I want; that's just exactly; that's what I want."
And I walked off the platform, you know, saying, "My." Said, "You ain't got room for me to preach." I thought, "Woo, my, he's been to a fountain of youth somewhere." I thought, "I want that."
text_no:
Men han kom tilbake derfra og førte Ham tilbake over himmelen på denne måten, og ned gjennom den horisontale regnbuen ved Den andre komme. Og omtrent da han kom ned, ropte den gamle mannen, "Whoopie," hoppet opp og klikket hælene sammen, gikk snublende av plattformen og sa, "Dere har ikke nok plass her oppe for at jeg kan forkynne."
Jeg så på det og tenkte, "Bror, hvis Den Hellige Ånd vil få en gammel mann til å oppføre seg slik, hva ville Den gjøre for meg? Det er det jeg ønsker; det er akkurat; det er det jeg ønsker."
Og jeg gikk av plattformen, vet du, og sa, "Å, min." Han sa, "Dere har ikke plass til at jeg kan forkynne." Jeg tenkte, "Woo, min, han har funnet en ungdomskilde et sted." Jeg tenkte, "Det er det jeg vil ha."

text:
And that night out in the cornfield, I wanted to press my trousers, and I laid them between the two seats, you know, like that, seersuckers, and I put them down there, and I prayed. I said, "God, that's the most wonderful people; give me favor before them. Let me find some kind of grace like that; they got what I'm wanting."
So I remember, the next morning I washed up and come in; it was about ten o'clock. I could've eat with them, but I couldn't put in nothing in their offering. So I didn't want to eat with them. I had my buns, or rolls. And I come on in, you know, and got me a good drink at a hydrant, drove my old Ford up and stopped, and went in. And they were singing that little song they sing and clapping their hands, singing, "I Know It Was The Blood; I Know It Was The Blood." And oh, every one of them just a screaming and a running. I thought, "Well, what do you know about this?"
text_no:
Den kvelden ute på åkeren ønsket jeg å presse buksene mine, så jeg la dem mellom de to setene, slik, seersuckers, og jeg la dem der og ba. Jeg sa: "Gud, dette er de mest vidunderlige menneskene; gi meg velvilje hos dem. La meg finne nåde; de har det jeg søker."
Neste morgen vasket jeg meg og kom inn rundt klokken ti. Jeg kunne ha spist med dem, men jeg hadde ingenting å legge i offeret, så jeg ville ikke spise med dem. Jeg hadde med meg noen boller. Jeg kom inn, tok en god slurk vann fra en hydrant, kjørte min gamle Ford og stanset, og gikk inn. De sang den lille sangen de pleide å synge mens de klappet i hendene, "I Know It Was The Blood; I Know It Was The Blood." Alle var henrykte, ropte og løp rundt. Jeg tenkte, "Hva i all verden er dette?"

text:
And I sat down by a colored brother. And I sat down there; they had the conference up in the North. So they couldn't have it in the South on account of the mixing of the colored and the white. And so, I sat down by a colored brother there. I had on a little T-shirt, you know. Nobody knowed me, so and a pair of seersucker trousers. I was sitting there listening, and a man, I believe, from Cincinnati by the name of Kirks, and he came out. Now, he belongs to one of those Pentecostal organizations, don't know which one it was, but best I remember, the man's tabernacle is named Raugh, R-a-u-g-h, a German, Raugh, Raugh, or something like that. And it was a… I got sat down there, you know, and I thought, "I'm going to enjoy this well today."
So this minister walked out; he said, "Last night on the platform there was a young preacher here, I believe the youngest we had in the audience; his name is Branham. Billy Branham," said, "If he's in the audience? Well, we want him to bring the message this morning."
text_no:
Jeg satte meg ned ved siden av en farget bror. De hadde konferansen i nord, siden de ikke kunne ha den i sør på grunn av blandingen av fargede og hvite. Jeg satt der i en liten T-skjorte og et par seersucker-bukser. Ingen kjente meg, så jeg satt der og lyttet. En mann ved navn Kirks fra Cincinnati, som tilhører en av de pinseorganisasjonene (jeg vet ikke hvilken), kom ut. Jeg tror menigheten hans heter Raugh, R-A-U-G-H, en tysk menighet. Jeg tenkte, "Dette skal jeg nyte i dag."
Denne forkynneren kom ut og sa: "I går kveld på plattformen var det en ung predikant her, jeg tror han var den yngste i publikum; hans navn er Branham. Billy Branham," sa han, "Hvis han er i publikum, vil vi at han skal bringe budskapet i morges."

text:
My, I hunkered down real low so, seersucker trousers and T-shirt you know; so I got down real low, you know. And he announced again, said, "Anybody outside know anywhere of Billy Branham is, from Indiana, down at Jeffersonville." Said, "Tell him to come to the platform."
Oh, I wasn't going up there before all them preachers like that. And I… Why, I couldn't preach, my old sassafras, slow, Baptist ways couldn't think of it that fast, so, way them fellows was preaching. I just set real still like this. And so, got down low in my seat. This colored brother looked over, said, "Say, you know who that guy is?"
Oh, I was on a spot. I said, I had to say something then. I said, "Look fellow, look; I'm he, you see," but I said, "don't tell them people." I said, "Look, I got on these seersucker trousers here, and this T-shirt."
He said, "Them people don't care what you dress like; get on up there."
And I said, "No, I can't go up; you keep still."
He said, "Anybody know what … and the where, Reverend Branham?"
That colored brother said, "Here he is!" Whew. "Here he is." Seersucker trousers, T-shirt.
Said, "Come on up, Mr. Branham."
Oh, my, I looked back at that brother, and he's just laughing, you know.
text_no:
Jeg satte meg lavt ned med mine seersucker-bukser og T-skjorte, du vet, så jeg satte meg virkelig lavt. Han annonserte igjen og sa: "Er det noen utenfor som vet hvor Billy Branham fra Indiana, Jeffersonville, er? Si til ham at han skal komme opp på plattformen."
Å nei, jeg ville ikke gå opp foran alle de forkynnerne. Jeg kunne ikke preke slik som dem med mine sakte, baptistiske måter; jeg klarte ikke å tenke så raskt som de. Så jeg satte meg helt stille. Jeg sank lavt ned i stolen. Denne mørkhudede broren så over og sa: "Si meg, vet du hvem den fyren er?"
Jeg var virkelig i et dilemma. Jeg måtte si noe. Jeg sa: "Se her, kompis; det er meg, skjønner du, men ikke fortell det til de andre." Jeg sa: "Se, jeg har på meg disse seersucker-buksene og denne T-skjorten."
Han sa: "Folkene bryr seg ikke om hvordan du er kledd; gå opp der."
Jeg sa: "Nei, jeg kan ikke gå opp; vær stille."
Han sa: "Er det noen som vet hvor Reverend Branham er?"
Den mørkhudede broren sa: "Her er han!" Puh. "Her er han." Seersucker-bukser og T-skjorte.
De sa: "Kom opp, Mr. Branham."
Å, jeg så tilbake på broren, og han lo, du vet.

text:
I started, and I thought, "Lord, I prayed, now what am I going to say when I get up there?" I started sneaking up the platform, my ears real red, you know. And I thought, "What am I going to do?" And I was afraid of that microphone hanging down there, you know. They had a hanging on a wire hanging down. And I got the Bible, and I was trembling so I couldn't hardly take a hold of it. I remember I got my text from, "The Rich Man Lifted Up His Eyes In Hell And Then He Cried," and then he cried, and then I cried. Ha-ha-ha. Something got a hold of me. I didn't know nothing for about a half hour; they had me outside. And all the screaming I ever heard in my life.
Here come a fellow up from Texas, had on cowboy boots and a great big hat, you know, said, "Say, I'm--I'm--I'm preacher so…" Well, I thought, "Them seersucker trousers' not so bad after all, then. I looked at him; he said, "I heard you was a evangelist; I want you to come hold me a revival in Texas."
And another fellow walked up, had on these little old golf pants, you know, that you, knicker-bocker pants. He said, "I'm from Florida; I have a big bunch--a church down there with so many people in it, will you come?"
Well, I thought, "Well, my T-shirt's not so bad, these are just home folks." So I believe…
text_no:
Jeg begynte å tenke, "Herre, jeg har bedt. Hva skal jeg si når jeg kommer opp der?" Jeg snek meg forsiktig opp på plattformen, og ørene mine var helt røde. Jeg tenkte, "Hva skal jeg gjøre?" Jeg var redd for mikrofonen som hang ned fra en ledning. Jeg tok tak i Bibelen, men jeg skalv så mye at jeg nesten ikke klarte å holde den. Jeg husker at jeg valgte teksten, "Den rike mannen løftet øynene sine i helvete og gråt", og så gråt han, og så gråt jeg. Ha-ha-ha. Noe fikk tak i meg. Jeg var uten bevissthet i omtrent en halv time; de hadde meg utenfor. Jeg hørte all den skrikingen jeg noen gang har hørt i mitt liv.
En kar fra Texas kom opp, med cowboyhatter og store støvler. Han sa, "Jeg er forkynner så..." Da tenkte jeg, "De seersucker-buksene er ikke så ille likevel." Jeg kikket på ham; han sa, "Jeg hørte at du var evangelist; jeg vil at du skal holde en vekkelse i Texas."
En annen mann kom, iført korte golfbukser, du vet, knickerbocker-bukser. Han sa, "Jeg er fra Florida; jeg har en stor menighet der nede med mange medlemmer. Kan du komme?"
Da tenkte jeg, "Vel, T-skjorten min er ikke så ille, dette er bare hjemmefolk." Så jeg tror…

text:
And a lady come up, she was teaching the Indians somewhere. And the first thing you know. I begin to put down invitations like that, and I had a whole line of them on the back of piece of paper. And I jumped in my old Ford, and down the road I went, my, oh, my. And I run home… When I got in… She's always was lovely, Brother Ryan, you know that. You… She would come always… She had long black hair, and she'd come running out the door to meet me, and she said, "You look so happy."
I said, "Honey, I found the greatest church in the world." I said, "A bunch of people that's not ashamed of their religion, they scream and shout and everything else."
She said, "Where you been?"
And I said, "Let's see, up around Mishawaka up there." I said, "Oh, you talk about a church," I said, "you never seen such. And believe it or not, let me show you something." I pulled out, I said, "I got an invitation to come to churches enough to keep me the whole year going."
She said, "You, honey?"
And I said, "Yep, me."
And she said, "Well…"
I said, "Would you go with me?"
She said, "I promised to go with you anywhere until death should separate us." That's a real wife. God rest her gallant soul today. Then said, "I'll go with you anywhere."
I said, "All right," I said, "Now, we'll go tell our parents."
text_no:
En dame kom opp; hun underviste indianerne et sted. Før jeg visste ordet av det, begynte jeg å notere invitasjoner, og jeg hadde en hel liste på baksiden av et papirark. Jeg hoppet inn i min gamle Ford og kjørte av gårde. Da jeg kom hjem... Hun har alltid vært herlig, Bror Ryan, det vet du. Hun hadde langt svart hår og pleide å komme løpende ut døra for å møte meg. Hun sa, "Du ser så glad ut."
Jeg sa, "Kjære, jeg har funnet den beste menigheten i verden. En gjeng mennesker som ikke skammer seg over sin religion, de skriker og roper og alt mulig."
Hun spurte, "Hvor har du vært?"
Jeg svarte, "La meg se, oppe rundt Mishawaka-området." Jeg sa, "Du kan ikke forestille deg en slik menighet. Tro det eller ei, la meg vise deg noe." Jeg dro fram papiret og sa, "Jeg har fått så mange invitasjoner til kirker at det vil holde meg opptatt hele året."
Hun sa, "Du, kjære?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja, meg."
Hun sa, "Vel..."
Jeg spurte, "Vil du bli med meg?"
Hun sa, "Jeg lovet å følge deg hvor enn det skulle være til døden skiller oss." Det er en ekte kone. Gud velsigne hennes edle sjel i dag. Så sa hun, "Jeg vil dra med deg hvor som helst."
Jeg sa, "Greit, nå går vi og forteller foreldrene våre."

text:
I went and told… She was to tell her mother. I told mama, and mama said, "Well," said, "Okay, Billy." Said, "I remember them kind of people down in Kentucky when I was a little girl," said, "they used to have the Old Lone Star meeting house up there." Said, "People get down at the altar, and they would pray and scream and run," that's old Missionary Baptist, and said, "They've got away from that today in these churches out here in Indiana, and around."
And that's a shame that we do it too. That's right. I tell you, this here kind of Baptist we have now that you shake hands and put your name on a paper; that's not the way I got it, brother. We got down at the altar and beat one another on the back, till we come through; we got something when we come out of there. Yes sir, it wasn't shaking hands with the preacher; we got saved.
text_no:
Jeg gikk og sa... Hun skulle fortelle moren sin. Jeg fortalte mamma, og mamma sa: "Vel, Billy," sa hun, "Jeg husker den typen mennesker fra Kentucky da jeg var liten jente," sa hun, "de pleide å ha det gamle Lone Star møteshuset der oppe." Hun sa: "Folk gikk ned til alteret, og de ba, skrek og løp rundt," det var de gamle Misjonsbaptistene, og sa, "I dag har menighetene her i Indiana og omegn fjernet seg fra det."
Det er synd at vi også har gjort det. Helt riktig. Jeg sier dere, denne typen baptist vi har nå, hvor man håndhilser og skriver navnet sitt på et papir; det er ikke måten jeg fikk det, bror. Vi gikk ned til alteret og slo hverandre på ryggen til vi kom igjennom; vi fikk noe da vi kom ut derfra. Ja, sir, det handlet ikke om å håndhilse på forkynneren; vi ble frelst.

text:
And then I remember, then when we started off, her mother said, "Well, Hope," she said, "You can go, of course, that's said, but I'm telling you," said, "if you do your mother will fill a grave with a broken heart."
Oh, my. There it was. And here's where I made my mistake, friends. I listened to my mother-in-law in the stead of listening to God. And if I would've went ahead then, this great thing would have been manifested before this, and the Church had been farther along. But listen to my mistakes; here's where sorrow started.
Hope said, "I'll go anyhow."
I didn't want to hurt her mother's feelings, and her mother said, "Why don't you go up there at the church until you get it paid for, and then get a parsonage, and act like somebody's got some sense, in the stead of [unclear words]. You think I could let my daughter be drug around across the country, and today she eats, and tomorrow she doesn't eat? And never have a change of clothes on her back," and said, "and drug around with that bunch of trash."
And I found out, and I don't say this, but nothing but to tell the truth. What she called the trash, I find to be the cream of the crop. That's exactly the truth. That's right.
Said, "My daughter be drug around such as that…"
Brother, by my mistakes we had to bury her a little after that.
text_no:
Jeg husker at, da vi startet, sa moren hennes, "Vel, Hope, du kan dra, men jeg sier deg, hvis du gjør det, vil moren din ende opp med et knust hjerte i en grav."
Å, min. Der var det. Og her gjorde jeg min feil, venner. Jeg lyttet til svigermoren min i stedet for å lytte til Gud. Hadde jeg gått videre da, ville denne store ting blitt manifestert tidligere, og Menigheten hadde kommet lengre. Men lær av mine feil; her begynte sorgen.
Hope sa, "Jeg drar uansett."
Jeg ønsket ikke å såre moren hennes, og hun sa, "Hvorfor går dere ikke opp til kirken til dere har betalt den, får en prestebolig, og oppfører dere som noen med fornuft, i stedet for [ulydelige ord]? Tror du jeg kan la datteren min bli dradd rundt over hele landet, hvor hun i dag spiser, men ikke i morgen? Og aldri har skiftet klær på ryggen," og sa, "og bli dradd rundt med den gjengen med søppel."
Og jeg fant ut, og jeg sier dette bare for å være ærlig. Det hun kalte søppel, har jeg funnet ut er krem av avlingen. Det er nøyaktig sannheten. Det er riktig.
Hun sa, "Min datter bli dratt rundt slik som det..."
Bror, på grunn av mine feil måtte vi begrave henne litt etter det.

text:
Now, I never forget; trouble started when… My father took sick, died on my arm just a little bit after that. My brother was riding on the side of a car, fifteen years old. The man that he was hitch hiking and picked him up, was drinking, hit his head up side of a post, broke his neck, turned his liver over, and he died in my other brothers arms. And I was standing in the platform preaching when it happened. They come and told me. There, I tell you, the way of a transgressor's hard. Don't you pay any attention to what anybody in the world tells you; you do what God says do, no matter what it… I don't care if it's…
One person come to me not long ago and said, "Brother Branham, I have 'Thus saith the Lord'; I know the Lord wants you to quit doing this and quit doing that."
I said, "Now, my brother, I love you with all my heart, but don't come to me with that." I said, "Because that's not Scriptural."
She said, "But I'm a prophet also."
I said, "If God… I'm on speaking terms with Him, if He wants me to know anything He will tell me." That's right. I said, "Well, there was one time there was two--two prophets. One of them went down, and he was young prophet, and he prophesied against the alt… I believe it's I Kings 13, and he prophesied against the altar and healed the kings arm after he'd been paralyzed. And another prophet said, "The Lord said for you to come by my way," after the Lord told him to do something else. And two prophets, you remember that? No matter who it's a prophet, or who it is, when God tells you do anything, you do what God says do. Let everything else go (See?); you just mind God.
text_no:
Jeg glemmer aldri; trøbbelet begynte da... Min far ble syk og døde i armene mine kort tid etter. Min bror, som var femten år gammel, satt på siden av en bil. Mannen som tok ham på da han haiket, var beruset og kjørte inn i en stolpe. Han brakk nakken og fikk leveren skadet. Min bror døde i armene på en annen bror av meg, mens jeg sto på plattformen og forkynte. De kom og fortalte meg hva som hadde skjedd. Jeg sier deg, det er hardt å være en overtreder. Ikke bry deg om hva noen i verden sier; gjør det Gud sier du skal gjøre, uansett hva som skjer... Jeg bryr meg ikke om hvem det er...
En person kom til meg for ikke lenge siden og sa, "Bror Branham, jeg har 'Så sier Herren'; jeg vet Herren vil at du skal slutte med dette og slutte med det."
Jeg sa, "Nå, min bror, jeg elsker deg av hele mitt hjerte, men ikke kom til meg med det der." Jeg sa, "Fordi det ikke er i samsvar med Skriften."
Hun sa, "Men jeg er også en profet."
Jeg sa, "Hvis Gud... Jeg er på talefot med Ham. Hvis Han vil at jeg skal vite noe, vil Han fortelle meg." Det er riktig. Jeg sa, "Vel, det var en gang det var to - to profeter. En av dem gikk ned og han var en ung profet, og han profeterte mot alteret, tror det står i 1. Kongebok 13. Han profeterte mot alteret og helbredet kongens arm etter at den var blitt lammet. En annen profet sa, 'Herren sa at du skal komme via min vei,' etter at Herren hadde sagt at han skulle gjøre noe annet. To profeter, husker du det? Uansett hvem som er profet, eller hvem det er, når Gud sier at du skal gjøre noe, gjør det Gud sier du skal gjøre. La alt annet fare; følg bare Gud."

text:
And there was sympathy and feeling; I said, "Well, I tell you honey, we'll just let the thing go, and then we'll go ahead and…" She said, "Bill, I'll go with you; I'll do anything that you want to do."
I said, "Well, we'll just let it go, and we'll--we'll--we'll finish paying for our church, and maybe after while we--we'll be able to go." And sorrow set in, one thing after another; trouble set in, everything.
Now remember, then immediately that 1937 flood came up. I remember poor old Brother Ryan was in the city at that time, how the flood coming up, and the workers on the wall… I had an old boat there, and used to go out there. And you'd get in that boat, and stand in that boat, and float down the river, and preach to them people on the flood walls. Then over back up the river, and then come back down the river preaching to the people on the flood wall. I remember the last time I seen you, Brother Ryan, there. I thought you were gone and in eternity here a few weeks, a few months ago. I never knowed what become of him.
text_no:
Det var medfølelse og følelse; jeg sa, "Vel, jeg sier deg, kjære, vi lar det ligge, og så fortsetter vi og..." Hun sa, "Bill, jeg blir med deg; jeg gjør hva du vil." 
Jeg sa, "Vel, vi lar det ligge, så--så--så betaler vi ned kirken vår, og kanskje etter en stund kan vi dra." Og sorg kom, én ting etter en annen; problemer kom, alt sammen.
Husk nå, da kom flommen i 1937. Jeg husker stakkars gamle Bror Ryan var i byen på den tiden, hvordan flommen steg, og arbeiderne på muren… Jeg hadde en gammel båt der, og pleide å dra ut. Du ville gå i båten, stå i den, og flyte nedover elven, og preke til folkene på flommurene. Så dra tilbake oppover elven, og så igjen nedover elven og preke til folket på flommuren. Jeg husker siste gang jeg så deg, Bror Ryan, der. Jeg trodde du var gått til evigheten for noen uker, noen måneder siden. Jeg visste ikke hva som hendte med ham.

text: And little old Brother George, the night come, I remember it was just day before Christmas; wife went over the river to get the kiddies Christmas presents. During that time, been another year, and a little over a year, about a year and eleven months it was, between… It was eleven months between the two children; a little girl was born. I called her Sharon Rose, after the Bible, the Rose of Sharon. Sweetest little thing, she just got big enough till she could just goo, and she was so sweet. And I just love little kiddies.
text_no: Og lille Bror George, det var kvelden før jul. Kona mi dro over elven for å hente julegaver til barna. Det hadde gått litt over ett år, cirka ett år og elleve måneder mellom de to barna. En liten jente ble født, og jeg kalte henne Sharon Rose, etter Bibelens Rose av Sharon. Hun var den søteste lille tingen, akkurat stor nok til å kunne pludre, og hun var så søt. Jeg elsker små barn.

text:
And then, I remember the flood came along, and she went over the river to get some things for the kiddies for Christmas, and I was working. And I come home, and there she'd fainted on the street and they'd brought her in. And I rushed in, looked at her, and I… Little friend of mine, Doctor Sam Adair at Jeffersonville, I thinks one of the best doctors in the world. We were schooled together, and buddied together, fish together, and you know. We did run around together. Live neighbors right now. And he… I called him; I'd just taken him down one of those--a lamp for Christmas, for a Christmas present; it was night, day before Christmas. And I called him, I said, "Sam, Hope's fainted." And I said…
He said, "I'll be right up, Bill."
He come up, and said, "Oh, my, she got a fever of a hundred and five; she's got pneumonia." And said, "You'll have to stay up all night, Bill, and give her fluids to drink."
Well, I did. And that night I knelt down and begin to pray, and pray for God to help her. And when I did, I was in prayer; I seen a black sheet come down before me. I seen the 1937 flood rise and twenty-two feet come over Spring Street, begin to prophesy. People said, "You're crazy; you're off at your head." Down at Falls City Transfer Company when I gave that, down there to them, they said, "Oh, Billy, go on home."
But less than six weeks from then, twenty-two feet of water measured over Spring Street, just exactly the way it said.
text_no:
Jeg husker da flommen kom. Hun dro over elva for å hente noen ting til barna til jul, og jeg var på jobb. Da jeg kom hjem, hadde hun besvimt på gaten og blitt brakt inn. Jeg skyndte meg inn og så på henne... En god venn av meg, Doktor Sam Adair i Jeffersonville, som jeg anser som en av de beste legene i verden. Vi gikk på skole sammen, var venner, fisket sammen, og holdt sammen. Vi bor fortsatt i nærheten av hverandre.
Jeg ringte ham. Jeg hadde nettopp gitt ham en lampe i julegave. Det var kvelden før jul. Jeg sa til ham, "Sam, Hope har besvimt."
Han svarte, "Jeg kommer med en gang, Bill."
Da han kom, sa han, "Å, hun har en feber på hundre og fem; hun har lungebetennelse. Du må være våken hele natten, Bill, og gi henne væske."
Det gjorde jeg. Den natten knelte jeg ned og begynte å be, ba Gud om hjelp. Mens jeg ba, så jeg et svart teppe falle ned foran meg. Jeg så flommen i 1937 stige og dekke Spring Street med tjue-to fot vann. Jeg begynte å profetere. Folk sa, "Du er gal; du har mistet forstanden." På Falls City Transfer Company, da jeg fortalte dem det, sa de, "Å, Billy, gå hjem."
Men mindre enn seks uker senere målte vannet tjue-to fot over Spring Street, akkurat som jeg hadde sagt.

text:
And there, I seen this sheet fall, and I went to my church; I said, "I believe that my disobedience has brought sorrow to my heart. My wife was going to go."
And they said, "Oh," said, "that's just your feeling for your wife."
She got worse. The flood came in, broke through that night, that terrible night, Brother Ryan. You remember when the people were walking the streets and crying and everything. And I had a little old patrol wagon there, that I was working with, trying to get people out of the flood. And I went down, and there was a… My wife had been moved out to the temporary hospital at the government depot; everything else was all under flood. And I went out there to see her; and I was looking around. And I run into Brother George DeArk; he's in glory today. And he said, "I…" he said, "I just seen you down there by the United Brethren church."
I said, "Have you seen Brother Ryan?"
He said, "Down by the United Brethren Church."
And Brother George put his arms around me, said, "Brother Billy, if I never see you no more; I'll see you in the morning."
And that's our next meeting time; he left during time of the flood; in glory today. When he was dying he looked, when he said, "Oh, if I could only see Brother Bill one more time. Oh, if he could only be here." He said, "Oh, where are you at?" He looked at the window, said, "Oh, Jesus, I knew You would come." Threw his arms out and went to meet God.
text_no:
Der så jeg dette lakenet falle. Jeg gikk til menigheten min og sa: "Jeg tror at min ulydighet har brakt sorg til hjertet mitt. Min kone var på vei til å dra."
De svarte: "Å, det er bare følelsene dine for din kone."
Hun ble verre. Flommen kom inn og brøt gjennom den natten, den forferdelige natten, Bror Ryan. Du husker hvordan folk gikk i gatene og gråt og alt det. Jeg hadde en liten, gammel patruljevogn som jeg arbeidet med for å få folk ut av flommen. Jeg dro ned, og min kone hadde blitt flyttet til det midlertidige sykehuset på statens depot; alt annet var under vann. Jeg drog dit for å se henne, og mens jeg kikket rundt, møtte jeg Bror George DeArk; han er i herlighet i dag. Han sa: "Jeg så deg nettopp der nede ved United Brethren-menigheten."
Jeg spurte: "Har du sett Bror Ryan?"
Han svarte: "Der nede ved United Brethren-menigheten."
Bror George la armene rundt meg og sa: "Bror Billy, om jeg aldri får se deg igjen, så møtes vi om morgenen."
Det er vår neste møtetid; han forlot oss under flommen og er i herlighet i dag. Da han var i ferd med å dø, sa han: "Å, om jeg bare kunne se Bror Bill en gang til. Å, om han bare kunne være her." Han så ut av vinduet og sa: "Å, Jesus, jeg visste at Du ville komme." Han strakte armene ut og gikk for å møte Gud.

text:
Then I went on down, this flood was about ready to break through the dike up above there, at Chestnut Street. And some of them called me up, said, "Hurry down there right quick." I was raised on the river, and thought I was a pretty good boatman. I got my boat down there real quick. They said, "A woman's trapped out there." And I looked out there and heard a woman screaming, standing out on top of a porch with a baby in her arms screaming, "Have mercy, have mercy," the house shaking like that, and the flood coming down, sweeping through, eighty mile stretch of water through there.
And I got my boat and started up through the alleys of the… Like this, and got it so I could get down and fall in behind the place in the current. And I landed against the place; and the woman had fainted; I picked her up and got her in the boat (About eleven o'clock that night.), two or three little girls. I got back away and got to the bank. When she come to herself, she kept hollering, "My baby, my baby, oh, don't leave my baby." And I thought she had a little bitty baby over there. The baby she had was about two years old. And I took back to try to see if we'd left a little baby laying on a bed or something, because she fainted on the porch when I picked her up.
text_no:
Deretter fortsatte jeg nedover. Flommen var i ferd med å bryte gjennom dikene ved Chestnut Street. Noen ringte meg og sa: "Kom ned hit straks." Jeg vokste opp ved elven og mente jeg var en ganske god båtfører. Jeg fikk båten min raskt på vannet. De sa: "En kvinne er fanget der ute." Jeg så en kvinne skrike, stående på en veranda med en baby i armene sine, og ropte "Ha nåde, ha nåde." Huset ristet, og flommen kom rasende, med vann som strakte seg over åtti mil.
Jeg tok båten og startet gjennom smugene på denne måten, slik at jeg kunne komme rundt og følge strømmen mot stedet. Jeg landet mot huset; kvinnen hadde besvimt. Jeg løftet henne opp og fikk henne inn i båten. (Det var omtrent klokken elleve den natten.) Jeg tok også med to eller tre små jenter. Vi kom oss tilbake til bredden. Da kvinnen kom til seg selv, ropte hun stadig: "Min baby, min baby, å, ikke forlat min baby." Jeg trodde hun refererte til en liten baby som lå igjen inne i huset. Babyen hun holdt var omtrent to år gammel. Jeg dro tilbake for å sjekke om vi hadde etterlatt en liten baby på en seng eller noe, fordi hun hadde besvimt på verandaen da jeg løftet henne opp.

text: And when I got there and tied my boat, and I went in and looked all through the house. But the baby she was talking about was a two year old baby. And just about that time the house washed from the foundation. And I ran out real quick and jumped in the boat, threw my hands down in the water and pulled the slip knot, ice, sleeting, cold, the wind blowing, blizzarding. And I got into the boat; I tried to pull the string on the starter. I couldn't get it started, and the current caught me this way, got me out onto Market Street, swept me out into the river. There's the Ohio Falls crushing just below there, the waves high as this building here, romping like that with such a current. And me standing in that boat pulling that string trying to get that motor to start, and it wouldn't start. Seemed like I could hear somebody say, "Now, where's that bunch of trash at?"
text_no: Da jeg kom dit og fortøyde båten, gikk jeg inn og undersøkte hele huset. Men babyen hun snakket om var en to år gammel baby. Akkurat da ble huset revet bort fra fundamentet. Jeg løp raskt ut, hoppet i båten, stakk hendene ned i vannet og løsnet knuten, i is, sludd, bitende kulde og kraftig vind. Jeg satte meg i båten og forsøkte å dra i startsnoren. Jeg fikk den ikke i gang, og strømmen tok meg med ut på Market Street, feide meg ut i elven. Der ligger Ohio Falls med bølger så høye som denne bygningen, brusende med en sterk strøm. Jeg sto i båten og trakk i snoren for å få motoren i gang, men den ville ikke starte. Det virket som om jeg kunne høre noen si: "Nå, hvor er den gjengen med søppel?"

text:
I tell you, brother, the way of a transgressor's hard. Don't you let no one call you trash, no. I pulled that string, and it wouldn't start, and I said, "Oh, God, I did that which is wrong. Please, I don't want to die out here in this river, my baby and wife laying out there very sick. Please God, don't let me die."
And I was pulling that string and it wouldn't start, and I turned around, and the boat was half full of water almost then, going on towards the falls. I know just ten minutes longer it'd be too much. I pulled again; I thought, "Oh, God, forgive me for my sins." And I pulled again; and when I pulled that time, the motor spluttered; I pulled again and it started.
I whirled around like that in the boat, got back, come out way down around by Cane Run Creek, down towards New Albany. I got back up to Jeffersonville again, walked back up. I said, "I'll go out and see my wife."
Said, "Your wife" said, "where's she at?"
I said, "The government hospital, out there."
Said, "That whole thing's washed away."
text_no:
Jeg sier deg, bror, at veien for en synder er hard. Ikke la noen kalle deg søppel. Jeg dro i snoren, og båtmotoren startet ikke. Jeg sa: "Å, Gud, jeg har gjort noe galt. Vær så snill, jeg vil ikke dø her ute i elven, min syke kone og baby ligger der ute veldig syke. Vær så snill, Gud, la meg ikke dø."
Jeg dro i snoren igjen, men motoren ville fortsatt ikke starte. Jeg snudde meg rundt, og båten var nesten halvfull av vann, på vei mot fossene. Jeg visste at om ti minutter ville det være for sent. Jeg dro igjen og ba: "Å, Gud, tilgi meg mine synder." Da jeg dro i snoren igjen, hostet motoren. Jeg dro en gang til, og den startet.
Jeg svingte båten rundt og klarte å komme meg ned langs Cane Run Creek mot New Albany. Jeg kom tilbake til Jeffersonville og gikk opp igjen. Jeg sa: "Jeg må ut og se min kone."
De sa, "Din kone? Hvor er hun?"
Jeg svarte, "På statens sykehus der ute."
De sa, "Hele området er skylt bort."

text:
Oh, my, then I jumped in my boat and ra--or my car, and run it as quick as I could, the little patrol truck I was in, and went out to the government out there. I met Major Weekly, a friend of mine, I said, "Major, is the hospital washed away?"
He said, "Yes, it's all under water down there, reverend," he said, "but I think all the patients got out."
I said, "Do you know where my wife did or not?"
Said, "I don't know."
And went and seen someone else who said, "Yes, your wife and all the rest of them got in a cattle car, and they've gone towards Charlestown."
text_no:
Å, Herre, da hoppet jeg inn i båten min og - eller bilen min, og kjørte så raskt jeg kunne, med den lille patruljebilen jeg hadde, og dro ut til myndighetene der. Jeg møtte major Weekly, en venn av meg, og spurte: "Major, er sykehuset vasket bort?"
Han svarte: "Ja, hele området er under vann der nede, pastor," sa han, "men jeg tror alle pasientene kom seg ut."
Jeg spurte: "Vet du om min kone kom seg ut?"
Han sa: "Det vet jeg ikke."
Jeg gikk for å spørre noen andre, og de sa: "Ja, din kone og alle de andre kom seg inn i en kvegetransport, og de har dratt mot Charlestown."

text:
Well, I run the car out towards Charlestown, the Lancassange Creek had backed up, four miles of the swiftest water. I went, got my boat. I couldn't pierce that stream to save me; it would fly back. I met some of them there, said, "You know, when they crossed that trestle over there, the train washed off the track," between Charlestown and Jeffersonville. And there me trying to get there, and got marooned out there by myself. And I had several days to set out there and think it all over.
Then when I could cross and get over again, I got to Charlestown; they said at there, "We hadn't heard nothing of my wife." I met an old friend of mine, we went walking down the street together; he said, "We will ask that dispatcher."
Dispatcher said, "Why, yes, I put off a sick mother and two children at Columbus, Indiana."
text_no:
Jeg kjørte bilen mot Charlestown, der Lancassange-bekken hadde steget og nådde fire mil av det raskeste vannet. Jeg hentet båten min, men klarte ikke å krysse strømmen; vannet presset tilbake. Jeg møtte noen der som sa: "Du vet, når de krysset den jernbanebrua der borte, ble toget vasket av sporet," mellom Charlestown og Jeffersonville. Der forsøkte jeg å komme frem, men ble fanget alene. Jeg hadde flere dager å sitte der og tenke over alt.
Da jeg endelig kunne krysse og komme tilbake, nådde jeg Charlestown. De der sa: "Vi har ikke hørt noe om kona di." Jeg møtte en gammel venn, og vi gikk sammen nedover gaten. Han sa: "Vi kan spørre den toglederen."
Toglederen sa: "Ja, jeg satte av en syk mor og to barn i Columbus, Indiana."

text:
And when a friend picked me up and took me to Columbus, then when I run into the Baptist church there, the gymnasium room being used for--a auditorium there for sick--where they put their sick. I went in there and the people crying and walking over each other, and the little old cots setting around. And I begin to scream; I got frantic. And I begin to scream, "Oh, Hope, where are you sweetheart? Where are you?" And I was running through there, "Where are you?"
And way back over in a corner, I'll never forget it; I seen a bony hand raise up like that [Blank spot on tape.] … my darling dying. I went over there to her real quick. I looked down at her, those dark eyes was sunk way back in her head, and her beautiful smooth face had drawed in. And she looked at me. And oh, I couldn't stand it. I just sunk down the side of the bed, and I said, "Oh, God, have mercy."
And she said, "I look horrible, don't I?"
And I said, "No, you're all right, honey." I said, "Where's Billy Paul and the baby?"
Said, "They're in… Somebody's got them down here in a room."
I said, "Are they alive and all right?"
Said, "Yes."
And just then I felt somebody tap me on the shoulder, and it was the doctor, he said, "Reverend Branham?"
And I said, "Yes."
Said, "Aren't you a friend of Doctor Sam Adair?"
And I said, "Yes."
Said, "Come over here just a minute." I walked over, and he said, "Now, Reverend Branham, I want to give you the shock so you'll know," so you can get over it, said, "you wife has developed galloping tuberculosis; she can't live but just a little bit." Said, "She's dying now."
I said, "Doctor, that can't be so."
He said, "It is so." Said, "Now, don't you let her know it, but you just go on, because Doctor Adair knows about it, and told me to tell you, he didn't want to tell you."
And I said, "All right."
text_no:
Da en venn hentet meg og tok meg med til Columbus, kom jeg inn i en baptistmenighet der gymnastikksalen ble brukt som et auditorium for syke. Jeg gikk inn, og folk gråt og snublet over hverandre, mens små gamle senger sto rundt omkring. Jeg begynte å rope, helt panisk: "Å, Hope, hvor er du, kjære? Hvor er du?" Jeg løp gjennom rommet og ropte: "Hvor er du?"
Langt bak i et hjørne, det glemmer jeg aldri, så jeg en beinete hånd løfte seg. [Tomt område på lydbånd.] ... min kjære som døde. Jeg hastet over til henne. Jeg så ned på henne; de mørke øynene hennes var sunket dypt inn i hodet, og det vakre, glatte ansiktet hennes hadde falt sammen. Hun så på meg, og jeg orket ikke mer. Jeg sank sammen ved siden av sengen og sa: "Å, Gud, ha miskunn."
Hun sa: "Jeg ser forferdelig ut, gjør jeg ikke?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, du er helt i orden, kjære." Jeg spurte: "Hvor er Billy Paul og babyen?"
Hun sa: "Noen har dem her nede i et rom."
Jeg spurte: "Er de i live og har det bra?"
Hun svarte: "Ja."
Akkurat da følte jeg noen tappe meg på skulderen. Det var legen. Han sa: "Pastor Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
Han fortsatte: "Er du venn med doktor Sam Adair?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
Han sa: "Kom bort hit et øyeblikk." Jeg gikk bort til ham, og han sa: "Pastor Branham, jeg ønsker å forberede deg på en sjokknyhet, så du kan bearbeide det. Din kone har utviklet galopperende tuberkulose; hun har bare kort tid igjen å leve." Han sa: "Hun dør nå."
Jeg svarte: "Doktor, det kan ikke være sant."
Han sa: "Det er sant." Han fortsatte: "Du må ikke la henne få vite det, men bare fortsett. Doktor Adair vet det, og ba meg fortelle deg fordi han ikke ønsket å gjøre det selv."
Jeg svarte: "Greit."

text:
And I went back over there knowing she was going, and oh my… And I asked the doctor, after it dried up and I could take her home?
